content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

message.content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the prompt file to understand the exercise.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### JSON Schema (normative)

The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.

**Input (stdin):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
  "properties": {
    "customer": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
      "properties": {
        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote step",
            "required": ["op", "items"],
            "properties": {
              "op": { "const": "quote" },
              "items": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["type"],
                  "properties": {
                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim step",
            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
            "properties": {
              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
              "incident": {
                "type": "object",
                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
                "properties": {
                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
                  "damages": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {
                      "type": "object",
                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
                      "properties": {
                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Output (stdout):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["results"],
  "properties": {
    "results": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote result",
            "required": ["premium"],
            "properties": {
              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim result",
            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
            "properties": {
              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

Read that file in full — it contains all rules, examples, integration examples, and the CLI JSON input/output schema.

Key behaviors to cover (derive the test list from the spec's examples, simple → complex):
- Item base premiums (sword/amulet/staff/potion, components at 25 G each)
- Building block of exactly 3 alike components (60 G)
- "Alike" = same type (separate blocks per type)
- Premium modifiers: cursed +50%, high enchantment (>=5) +30%, loyalty (>=2 yrs) -20%, first insurance +10%, follow-up contract -15%, +5 G processing fee
- Modifier scope: item-specific modifiers on item base premium; policy-wide on policy total; fee last
- Rounding in MHPCO favor (premium up, payout down; intermediate kept as fractions)
- Claim processing: 100 G deductible per damage event, cap = 2x insurance sum, enchantment>=8 -> 50%, dragon material -> full, cap exhaustion across successive claims
- Edge cases: empty item list, unknown item type, claim referencing item not in policy, negative damage amount, more damages of a type than insured -> non-zero exit
- CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout (results array)

Note there is a separate CLI entry point required at src/cli.ts, but the core logic should be in src/claim-office.ts and tested in src/claim-office.spec.ts.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
const basePremiumByType: Record<string, number> = { amulet: 60, sword: 100, staff: 80 };
const type = items[0]?.type;
const basePremium = basePremiumByType[type ?? ""];
return { basePremium, premium: 0 };
```

Component count:
- Constants: `60`, `100`, `80`, key strings `amulet`/`sword`/`staff` (3), `""`, index `0`, `premium: 0` = 3 + 3 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 9
- Bindings: `basePremiumByType`, `type`, `basePremium`, `items` = 4
- Invocations: `items[0]?.type` access (1), `basePremiumByType[...]` access (1) = 2 → mass 4
- Conditionals: `?? ""` nullish coalescing (1) = 1 → mass 4
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Total: constants 9 + bindings 4 + invocations 4 + conditionals 4 = **21**

Mass change: 20 → 21 (Δ +1)

## Step 6: Document Decision

The mass rose by 1 (the table declares more named constants), but **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**. The refactoring trades a nested ternary chain (`type === "sword" ? 100 : type === "staff" ? 80 : 60`) for a declarative lookup table where each item type maps explicitly to its base premium. Benefits:

- Each type→premium mapping is stated once, directly and readably (Rule 2).
- Eliminated the repeated `type ===` structural duplication and the two nested ternaries → 2 conditionals reduced to 1 (Rule 3).
- The implicit `: 60` magic default for "everything else" is gone; `amulet` is now explicit. Unknown types now yield `undefined` rather than silently returning 60 — which **preserves the Red signal** for upcoming `potion`, `rune`, and empty-list todos.

## Anticipation verification

I included only the three currently-tested types (`amulet`, `sword`, `staff`). I did NOT add `potion: 40`, `rune: 25`, or any block/modifier logic. When Red activates `potion → base premium 40`, the lookup returns `undefined`, the test fails — the cycle's Red signal is intact. No future behavior was implemented.

             todos still fail when activated.
```

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;   // module-level binding + constant
...
const premium = items.length === 0 ? PROCESSING_FEE : 0;
```

The ternary now references `PROCESSING_FEE` (a binding, mass 1) instead of literal `5` (a constant, mass 1) — same per-token mass. The added module constant adds 1 binding + 1 constant = +2 mass total.

- Function body: 22 (unchanged in structure; `5` literal → `PROCESSING_FEE` binding is mass-neutral)
- Module constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`: binding (1) + constant (1) = 2

Total Mass: 22 → **24** (Δ +2)

Mass increased slightly, but **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**. The named constant documents that the empty-list amount is a processing fee, not an arbitrary number.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted the magic number `5` into a named module constant `PROCESSING_FEE`, clarifying that the empty-list premium represents a processing fee rather than an arbitrary value.

**Considered but rejected (anticipation guard):**
- Removing/generalizing the `items.length === 0 ? ... : 0` ternary into real premium computation. Rejected — no active test demands premium math for non-empty lists. The modifier and integration tests that need it remain `it.todo()`. Generalizing now would make a future Red test pass immediately, breaking the cycle.

**Naming, Duplication, Fewest-Elements:** All evaluated; existing names are clear, no duplication exists, and element count is already minimal for the current test set.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```

Refactoring: Extracted magic number 5 into named constant PROCESSING_FEE (Rule 2 — reveals intent)
Mass Change: 22 → 24 (Δ +2; accepted because clarity trumps APP)

No future `it.todo()` (curse surcharge, enchantment, loyalty) is exercised by this grouping logic, so the Red signal for upcoming cycles stays intact.

It deliberately does NOT generalize to a per-item cursed surcharge across a policy — test 17 (cursed sword + plain amulet → 210) remains `it.todo()` and would still fail under this fake (the multi-item path returns the unsurcharged base). This preserves the Red signal for that future test rather than letting it pass for free.

content.text:
All 13 tests still green.

## Step 5: Mass change for the touched lines

**Before:**
```ts
const hasNoItems = items.length === 0;          // binding(1) + .length invocation(2) + ===(2) + const 0(1) = 6
const premium = hasNoItems ? PROCESSING_FEE : 0; // binding premium(1) + conditional(4) + binding hasNoItems(1) + binding PROCESSING_FEE(1) + const 0(1) = 8
                                                 // subtotal = 14
```

**After:**
```ts
const premium = items.length === 0 ? PROCESSING_FEE : 0;
// binding premium(1) + .length invocation(2) + ===(2) + const 0(1) + conditional(4) + binding PROCESSING_FEE(1) + const 0(1) = 12
```

Mass change for these lines: **14 → 12 (Δ −2)**, by eliminating one single-use intermediate binding (Rule 4: fewest elements).

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): Inlined the single-use `hasNoItems` boolean into the `premium` expression. `items.length === 0` is self-explanatory, so the named intermediate added an element without adding clarity. Mass −2.

**Deliberately NOT done (anticipation guard):**
- Did NOT generalize the cursed branch `items.length === 1 && items[0].cursed`. The not-yet-active test on line 102 (cursed surcharge on a multi-item policy) demands per-item cursed handling. Generalizing now would make that future Red test pass immediately, collapsing the TDD signal. The current narrow guard correctly reflects exactly what the 13 active tests prove.
- Naming already reveals intent across the board; no renames warranted.
- No real duplication to extract.

## Step 7: Completion

```
Refactor Phase Complete:
Refactoring: Inlined single-use `hasNoItems` boolean (Rule 4, fewest elements)
Mass Change: touched lines 14 → 12 (Δ −2)
Tests: All 13 passing
```

content.text:
Green Phase Complete.

**Implementation**: Changed the surcharge calculation in `quote` from a single-item-only factor to a per-item summation. Each item's surcharge is `basePremiumByType[item.type] * (surchargeFactorFor(item) - 1)`, summed across all items and added to the block-aware base. For the failing test: cursed sword contributes `100 * 0.5 = 50`, plain amulet contributes `0`, so basePremium = 160 (blocks/base) + 50 = 210.

**Result**: All tests now pass (17 passing, 23 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal per R5 — the surcharge is item-specific and applies to the affected item's own base premium. The per-item generalization was forced because a 2-item policy cannot pass via a literal branch without effectively summing per-item surcharges. It correctly subsumes the single-item cases (cursed → 150, ench5 → 130 still green) since a one-item policy sums exactly one surcharge. No policy-wide modifiers, fee changes, or claim logic were added.

Expected behavior: premium for {yearsWithMHPCO:3}, [cursed sword ench 7], followUp:true = 160.

The full additive formula (per spec R4/R5/R8): start from policy base premium P = sum of item base premiums BEFORE item surcharges (here sword = 100).
- item-specific surcharges (on the affected item's base): cursed +50% of that item's base, high-enchantment (>=5) +30% of that item's base
- policy-wide modifiers (each computed as a percentage of the policy base P, added/subtracted additively, NOT compounded): loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO>=2) -20% of P; first insurance +10% of P (always applies — every quote item is treated as a first insurance); follow-up contract (when followUp option true) -15% of P
- then + 5 processing fee
- round in MHPCO favor: premium rounds UP (Math.ceil), intermediates kept as fractions

Check against the active premium tests:
- test 16 loyalty: {2 yrs}, sword, followUp:false → 100 -20 +10 +5 = 95 ✓
- test 18 newcomer: {0 yrs}, cursed sword ench3, followUp:false → 100 +50 curse +10 first-ins +5 = 165 ✓
- test 19: {3 yrs}, cursed sword ench7, followUp:true → 100 +50 curse +30 high-ench -20 loyalty +10 first-ins -15 follow-up +5 = 160 ✓
- empty list → 5 ✓

Replace the hardcoded premium ternary with this real computation. Keep ALL 18 prior tests green (including the basePremium-only assertions which must be unaffected).
Current error: expected 95 to be 160
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

content.text:
Green Phase Complete.

**Implementation**: Replaced the hardcoded premium ternary in `quote` with the real additive formula. Policy-wide modifiers are each computed as a percentage of the policy base P (= `baseWithoutSurcharges`) and added/subtracted additively: loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO>=2) −20%, first-insurance (non-empty list) +10%, follow-up (option) −15%; then `Math.ceil(premium + 5)` for the processing fee with round-up in MHPCO favor. I also had to generalize `surchargeFactorFor`'s `if/else` early-return (a fake from cycles 13/14, where only one surcharge was ever present at a time) into an additive `surchargeRateFor` so a cursed AND high-enchantment item stacks +50% +30% on the same item base.

**Result**: All tests now pass (19 passing, 21 todo).

**Approach**: This was the cycle where the formula could no longer be faked. Three distinct premium values (95, 165, 160) across three distinct modifier combinations plus the empty-list case (5) cannot be branched-on cleanly without effectively encoding the formula. The surcharge generalization was forced specifically by test 19: it is the first test combining `cursed` + `enchantment>=5` on one item, which broke the prior single-surcharge fake — exactly the "generalize only when a test breaks the fake" trigger. Tests 13 and 14 still verify each surcharge in isolation and remain green; all 12 basePremium-only assertions are unaffected since `basePremium` continues to be returned unchanged.

## Refactoring Summary

**Naming Evaluation (First Priority):** All function and variable names (`quote`, `surchargeRateFor`, `surchargeFor`, `premiumForTypeBlock`, `baseWithoutSurcharges`, `countByType`) already clearly reveal intent. No renames needed.

**Refactoring Applied — Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):**
Extracted three magic literals in `surchargeRateFor` into named constants:
- `0.5` → `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`
- `5` → `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`
- `0.3` → `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`

This matches the existing convention already used in the file (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`, `FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE`, etc.). The surcharge rule now reads as named business policy rather than bare numbers, and the `>= 5` enchantment boundary (a tested edge: 5 triggers, 4 does not) is now self-documenting.

**APP Mass — `surchargeRateFor`:**
- Before: bindings `rate`(1) + 2 literals (`0.5`,`0.3` = 2) + literal `5`(1) + binding `item`(implicit param) + 2 conditionals (8) + `??` invocation-ish + 2 assignments (`+=` ×2 = 12) + literal `0` (1). Approx mass 25.
- After: literals replaced by named bindings — mass is essentially unchanged (constant → binding, both mass 1). Net Δ ≈ 0.

Mass was deliberately not the optimization target here — **Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP**. The change improves intent-revelation at neutral mass cost.

**Considered and rejected (would harm Rule 2 or anticipate):**
- Collapsing the three policy modifiers (loyalty/first-insurance/follow-up) into a loop over a rate table: would add a loop (mass 5) and obscure three distinct, individually-tested business rules. Rejected — clarity over mass.
- Touching `claim()` or any `claim`/CLI behavior: those are `it.todo()` only. Adding logic there would anticipate future tests. Left as the existing `undefined` stub.

**Tests:** 20 passing, 20 todo. All green.

🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "regular sword (steel, ench 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G (500 − 100 deductible)"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find / wrong-arity TypeScript error for `claim(policy, incident)` ❌ Incorrect
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 400, received 0 ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error (`expected +0 to be 400`)

Proceeding to Green phase.

---

Discrepancy explanation (Compilation Prediction was Incorrect): I predicted a TypeScript compilation error because `claim()` was declared with zero parameters and returned `unknown`, while the test calls `claim(policy, incident)` and reads `.payout`. However, the test runner is `vitest run`, which transpiles via esbuild and performs **no type checking**. So there was never a compile stage to fail: the two extra arguments were silently accepted, the stub returned `undefined`, and `.payout` on `undefined` threw a runtime `TypeError` immediately. I then added a minimal `claim(_policy, _incident): ClaimResult` stub returning `{ payout: 0 }`, which produced the clean two-stage assertion failure (Expected 400, Received 0) as predicted.

- Test file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_10-51-43_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts` (test 21 activated at lines 149-158)

content.text:
All 21 tests still passing.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
export function claim(_policy: QuoteResult, incident: Incident): ClaimResult {
  const damage = incident.damages[0];
  return { payout: damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE };
}
```

- Constants: `0` index = 1
- Bindings: `incident`, `damages`, `damage`, `amount`, `DEDUCTIBLE`, `payout` = 6
- Invocations: `-` = 1 × 2 = 2
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0 (`const` binding counts as a binding, not a mutation)

Total Mass: 1 + 6 + 2 = **9**

Mass Change: 8 → 9 (Δ +1)

## Step 6: Document Decision

The mass increased by 1 (one extra binding for the `damage` variable). This is the deliberate Rule 2 trade-off: **clarity trumps APP**. Naming the destructured damage entry `damage` makes the payout formula read as "the damaged item's amount minus the deductible" rather than relying on a bare `[0]` index in the middle of an arithmetic expression. The +1 mass is justified by the gain in intent revelation.

I deliberately did NOT:
- Add a loop over `incident.damages` (anticipates the multi-damage / per-item-deductible todos)
- Add 50%/dragon/high-enchantment clause branches (anticipates R7 clause todos)
- Add `Math.floor` rounding (anticipates the 350.5 rounding todo)
- Use the `_policy` parameter for caps (anticipates the caps/exhaustion todos)

Each of those would make a not-yet-active `it.todo()` pass immediately, breaking the next Red phase.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```
Refactor Phase Complete:
Refactoring: Extracted explaining variable `damage` for the indexed damage
             entry (Rule 2 — reveals intent). Kept `claim` name (correct
             domain term) and `_policy` unused-param marker (accurate for
             the single current test).
Mass Change: 8 → 9 (Δ +1) — accepted Rule-2-over-APP trade-off for clarity.
Tests: All 21 passing (19 todo remain).
```

input.prompt:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "dragon-material sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G (50% clause then deductible: 500 − 100)"
Expected behavior: For a damaged item with enchantment >= 8, the damage is reimbursed at 50% BEFORE the 100 deductible. Dragon sword ench 8, damage 1000: 1000 * 0.5 = 500, then -100 deductible = 400.

To do this, claim() must find the damaged item in the policy by itemType and read its enchantment. The QuoteResult currently only has {basePremium, premium}. You need to thread the quoted items into the policy object so claim() can look them up — enrich what quote() returns (e.g. add the items array to QuoteResult). The damage's itemType matches an item's type.

Keep all 22 prior tests green. In particular:
- test 21: steel sword ench 3, damage 500 → 400 (no clause: ench<8, not dragon → full minus deductible)
- test 22: rune damage 200 → 100 (rune has no enchantment/material → full minus deductible)
So the clause is: if matched item enchantment >= 8, payout = damage*0.5 - 100; otherwise payout = damage - 100. (Dragon material full-reimbursement is a SEPARATE later todo — do NOT implement dragon handling yet beyond what this test needs; here the dragon sword has ench 8 so the enchantment clause already produces 400. Don't add a dragon branch yet.)
Current error: expected 900 to be 400
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text:
🟢 Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added `items: Item[]` to `QuoteResult` and returned `items` from `quote()`. In `claim()`, look up the damaged item in `policy.items` by matching `type === damage.itemType`, then apply a single `if/else` branch: if that item's `enchantment >= 8`, halve the damage before subtracting the deductible; otherwise subtract the deductible from the full amount.

**Result**: All tests now pass (23 passing, 17 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal — a single conditional on the matched item's enchantment is exactly what this test demands. I deliberately did NOT add a dragon-material branch (the next todo at line 179 covers dragon full-reimbursement separately); here the dragon sword's ench-8 already triggers the enchantment clause to produce 400, so a dragon branch would be premature generalization. I also did not add per-item iteration over multiple damages (multi-damage is a later todo) — `damages[0]` still suffices.

Mechanical check: the next todo is "dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → 700". My impl gives `800 - 100 = 900` for that case (ench 5 < 8, no dragon branch), so it will correctly FAIL when Red activates it — no premature pass. Good signal preserved.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

content.text:
All 23 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
const reimbursed =
  (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD
    ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : damage.amount;
```

Counting the `claim` function body the same way (two literals `8` and `0.5` became named bindings; the two `const` declarations add 2 assignments at module scope, but within the function the literals are now binding references rather than constants — net component count for the function is unchanged):

- Constants: `0` = 1 (the `8` and `0.5` are now named bindings)
- Bindings: `policy`, `incident`, `damage`, `item`, `candidate`, `reimbursed`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` (ref), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE` (ref) = 8
- Invocations: `.find`, `===`, `??`, `>=`, `*`, `-` = 6 → mass 12
- Conditionals: ternary = 1 → mass 4
- Loops: `.find` = 1 → mass 5

Function-body Mass: (1×1) + (8×1) + (6×2) + (1×4) + (1×5) = 1 + 8 + 12 + 4 + 5 = **30**

Mass Change: 30 → 30 (Δ 0). Mass is unchanged — the two literals simply became named references. This is the correct outcome: per the guidelines, **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**, and the goal here was clarity, not mass reduction.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming (Rule 2): Extracted `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD = 8` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE = 0.5` from the bare literals on the reimbursement line.
- Benefit: The claim-side magic numbers now reveal intent and match the existing convention used throughout the `quote` side (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, etc.). A reader no longer has to infer what `8` and `0.5` mean.

**No anticipation**: I deliberately did NOT extract a generic reimbursement-clause engine, add dragon-material handling, multi-item iteration, caps, or rounding. Those are all `it.todo` entries with no active test. Introducing any of them would make a future Red phase pass immediately and break the TDD signal. The refactoring only renames literals the current passing test (`enchantment exactly 8 → 50% clause`) already exercises.

**Mass**: 30 → 30 (Δ 0). Naming clarity gained with no behavioral or structural change, consistent with Rule 2 taking priority over APP.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted two named constants (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`) to reveal intent of the claim-side magic numbers (Rule 2).
- **Mass Change**: 30 → 30 (Δ 0) — clarity improvement, no structural change.
- **Tests**: All 23 passing.
- **No anticipation**: Left all 17 `it.todo` behaviors unimplemented.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
const damage = incident.damages[0];
const damagedItem = policy.items.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);
const isHighEnchantment = (damagedItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD;
const reimbursed = isHighEnchantment
  ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
  : damage.amount;
return { payout: reimbursed - DEDUCTIBLE };
```

Component count:
- Constants: `0` (index), `0` (?? fallback) = 2

All 25 tests still green.

## Step 5: APP Mass Change

Mass is unchanged by this rename — same number of constants, bindings, invocations, conditionals (the rename does not add/remove elements). Mass ≈ unchanged. This was a Rule-2 (reveals intent) improvement, which trumps APP.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**
- Renamed `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` to `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`
- Renamed `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE` to `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`
- Renamed local `isHighEnchantment` to `halfReimbursementApplies`

Rationale: The quote side already has `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5) and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3). The claim-side constants previously echoed that "high enchantment" wording, blurring two distinct domain rules. The new names describe what the claim rule *does* (a half/50% reimbursement triggered at enchantment 8), making the distinct claim clause self-documenting and removing the cross-domain naming collision.

**Mass:** unchanged (pure rename, no element count change). Clarity prioritized over APP per the rules.

**Anticipation check passed:** No new branches, loops, material checks, rounding, or cap logic were introduced. The function still handles only `damages[0]` and the single enchantment clause. The upcoming todo tests (dragon+ench9 precedence, per-item deductibles, rounding, caps) will each still see a genuinely failing test in their Red phase.

## Step 7: Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring:** Renamed claim-side enchantment-clause constants/variable to reveal the distinct "half reimbursement at enchantment 8" intent and eliminate the naming collision with the quote-side high-enchantment surcharge.
- **Mass Change:** unchanged (rename only); Rule 2 prioritized over APP.
- **Tests:** 25 passing, 15 todo. All green.

content.text:
## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete**

**Refactoring**: None performed — every candidate was rejected as either a Rule 2 (clarity) regression or a no-anticipation violation.

**Naming Evaluation (first priority)**: `claim`, `damagedItem`, `halfReimbursementApplies`, `reimbursed`, and the threshold/rate constants all reveal intent. No rename improves clarity.

**Candidates considered and rejected**:
- Collapse the `reimbursed` ternary into a numeric multiplier → reduces clarity. **Rejected (Rule 2 trumps APP).**

The `claim` reduce body is now a single invocation (`payoutForDamage`) plus the `+`. The per-damage logic moved to a named helper. content.text:
All 28 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```typescript
export function claim(policy: QuoteResult, incident: Incident): ClaimResult {
  const totalPayout = incident.damages.reduce(
    (total, damage) => total + payoutForDamage(damage, policy),
    0,
  );
  return { payout: Math.floor(totalPayout) };
}
```

- Constants: `0` = 1
- Bindings: `policy`, `incident`, `totalPayout`, `total`, `damage` = 5 (removed `payout`)
- Invocations: `reduce`, `+`, `payoutForDamage`, `Math.floor` = 4 × 2 = 8
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: `reduce` = 5
- Assignments: 0

Total Mass: 1 + 5 + 8 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **19**

Mass Change: 20 → 19 (Δ −1)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Removed the redundant `payout` const binding by inlining `Math.floor(totalPayout)` directly into the returned object.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Preserved — kept `totalPayout` as a named intermediate so the "sum, then round down" intent stays readable. Did not collapse it into the return, which would have buried the reduce inside `Math.floor(...)` and hurt clarity.
- Mass: 20 → 19.

**No anticipation:** The change touches only the floor-then-return behavior the current passing test exercises. The 6 `it.todo` claim-cap tests and 6 CLI todos remain untouched — no cap logic, exhaustion tracking, or remainingCap was introduced.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** Inlined the redundant `payout` binding into the return statement (Rule 4 — fewest elements), keeping `totalPayout` named for intent clarity.
- **Mass Change:** 20 → 19 (Δ −1)

content.text:
All 28 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```typescript
export function claim(policy: QuoteResult, incident: Incident): ClaimResult {
  const totalPayout = incident.damages.reduce(
    (total, damage) => total + payoutForDamage(damage, policy),
    0,
  );
  return { payout: Math.floor(totalPayout) };
}
```

- Constants: `0` = 1
- Bindings: `policy`, `incident`, `totalPayout`, `total`, `damage` = 5 (removed `payout`)
- Invocations: `reduce`, `+`, `payoutForDamage`, `Math.floor` = 4 × 2 = 8
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: `reduce` = 5
- Assignments: 0

Total Mass: 1 + 5 + 8 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **19**

Mass Change: 20 → 19 (Δ −1)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Removed the redundant `payout` const binding by inlining `Math.floor(totalPayout)` directly into the returned object.

- Mass: 20 → 19.

**No anticipation:** The change touches only the floor-then-return behavior the current passing test exercises. The 6 `it.todo` claim-cap tests and 6 CLI todos remain untouched — no cap logic, exhaustion tracking, or remainingCap was introduced.

All 31 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The refactoring extracted `enchantmentLevel(item)`, replacing two inline `(item.enchantment ?? 0)` expressions (each: 1 binding + 1 invocation `??` + 1 constant `0` = mass 4 inline) with a single named helper plus two invocations.

**New `enchantmentLevel` helper:**
- Binding `item` = 1, invocation `??` = 2, constant `0` = 1 → mass 4

**At the two call sites:** each inline expression (mass ~4) becomes one invocation `enchantmentLevel(item)` (mass 2). Net per site: −2, total −4 at call sites; +4 for the new function definition.

Mass change: roughly neutral (≈ 0), with a small one-time +1 for the new binding name. APP is essentially unchanged — this refactoring is justified by **Rule 2 (reveals intent)** and **Rule 3 (removes conceptual duplication)**, which take priority over APP mass.

There is one behavioral note: in `payoutForDamage` the old `(damagedItem?.enchantment ?? 0)` collapsed the "item not found" case into level 0. The new code makes the `damagedItem !== undefined` guard explicit (adds a conditional, +4 mass) but reveals intent more clearly and preserves identical behavior for all current tests (every test damages an item that exists in the policy).

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Naming: All existing names already reveal intent — no rename needed.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Extracted the repeated `item.enchantment ?? 0` concept into a named `enchantmentLevel(item)` helper, used by both the surcharge logic (threshold 5) and the half-reimbursement logic (threshold 8).
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Made the "item must exist in policy" precondition explicit via `damagedItem !== undefined` rather than relying on optional-chaining coalescing to 0.

**No anticipation:** No new lookup-table entries, branches, or generalizations were added for the 9 pending `it.todo()` tests (successive claims / cap exhaustion, two-sword cases, CLI). The change only re-expresses logic the 31 active tests already exercise.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `enchantmentLevel(item)` helper to remove conceptual duplication (Rule 3) and made the damaged-item-exists precondition explicit (Rule 2).
- **Mass Change**: ≈ neutral overall (call sites −4, new helper +4, explicit guard +4); justified by clarity/duplication priority over APP.
- **Tests**: All 31 passing ✅ (9 todo untouched).

content.text:
All 33 tests pass. Now I'll work through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The most recent change relates to test 33 ("two swords → cap 4000"), which exercises `totalInsuranceValue`. Let me evaluate the naming of the functions involved.

- `totalInsuranceValue(policy)` — sums `insuranceValueByType` over all items. Name clearly reveals intent: it computes the total insurance value across all insured items. **Keep.**
- `remainingCapFor(policy)` — returns the stored remaining cap, or computes the initial cap (2× total insurance value). Name reveals intent. **Keep.**
- `payoutForDamage`, `surchargeFor`, `premiumForTypeBlock` — all descriptive and accurate. **Keep.**

No naming change warranted; names already reveal intent accurately for the behavior all 33 tests exercise.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on `totalInsuranceValue` (the function test 33 exercises) and the closely related `remainingCapFor`:

```
function totalInsuranceValue(policy: QuoteResult): number {
  return policy.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + insuranceValueByType[item.type], 0);
}
```
- Constants: `0` literal = 1
- Bindings: `policy`, `sum`, `item` = 3
- Invocations: `reduce`, `+`, index lookup `[]` = 3 → mass 6
- Loops: `reduce` = 5
- **Subtotal: 1 + 3 + 6 + 5 = 15**

```
function remainingCapFor(policy: QuoteResult): number {
  return policy.remainingCap ?? CAP_MULTIPLIER * totalInsuranceValue(policy);
}
```
- Bindings: `policy`, `CAP_MULTIPLIER` = 2
- Invocations: `*`, `totalInsuranceValue()` = 2 → mass 4
- Conditionals: `??` = 4
- **Subtotal: 2 + 4 + 4 = 10**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 33 pass. ✅
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names are clear and self-documenting. The `reduce` accumulator pattern is idiomatic and readable. No improvement available without harming clarity.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication):** `totalInsuranceValue` (reduce over `insuranceValueByType`) mirrors the `baseWithoutSurcharges` reduce over `basePremiumByType`, but they sum *different* tables for *different* purposes (premium vs. insurance value). This is incidental similarity, not conceptual duplication — extracting a shared "sum a lookup table over items" helper would be a generic abstraction that no current test demands, and would obscure the two distinct domain concepts. Forcing DRY here would violate Rule 2 (clarity) and constitute anticipation. **No extraction.**
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** The implementation is already minimal. No unnecessary abstractions, no dead code.

## Step 4: Refactoring Attempt

I considered extracting a generic `sumOverItems(items, table)` helper to unify `totalInsuranceValue` and `baseWithoutSurcharges`. I rejected it: it merges two distinct domain concepts behind one generic name (hurting Rule 2) and introduces a higher-order abstraction no test requires (anticipation / Rule 4 violation).

The code reached this state through prior refactor cycles and is already at a clean local optimum. No safe improvement remains that does not either reduce clarity or anticipate future tests.

## Step 5 & 6: Documentation

**Refactoring Evaluation:**
- ❌ Naming: `totalInsuranceValue`, `remainingCapFor`, and all helpers already reveal intent accurately for the behavior the 33 tests exercise.
- ❌ Duplication: The apparent similarity between `totalInsuranceValue` and `baseWithoutSurcharges` is incidental (different tables, different domain concepts). Merging would harm clarity and anticipate.
- ❌ Mass: Both functions are already minimal (15 and 10) for the work they do.
- ❌ Simplification: No unnecessary abstractions or dead code.

No code change performed — the implementation is already clean. (Note: I did not invent a refactoring that would degrade clarity or anticipate future tests merely to satisfy the "must change something" urge; per the rules, Rule 2 trumps APP and anticipation is forbidden.)

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** None possible — code already at a clean local optimum (see evaluation above).
- **Mass:** `totalInsuranceValue` = 15, `remainingCapFor` = 10 (unchanged; no change made).
- **Tests:** All 33 passing ✅

content.text:
All 34 tests still green.

## Step 5: APP Mass Comparison

**Before** — single `payoutForDamage`:
- Bindings: `damage`, `policy`, `damagedItem`, `item`, `halfReimbursementApplies`, `reimbursed` = 6
- Conditionals: `&&`, `?:` = 2 → mass 8
- Invocations: `find`, arrow, `enchantmentLevel`, `*`, `-` ≈ 5 → mass 10
- Constants: threshold, rate, deductible = 3
- Total ≈ 27

**After** — `reimbursedAmount` + `payoutForDamage`:
- `reimbursedAmount`: bindings 5, conditionals 2 (mass 8), invocations 4 (mass 8), constants 2 → ≈ 23
- `payoutForDamage`: binding `damage`,`policy` = 2, invocation `reimbursedAmount` + `-` = 2 (mass 4), constant `DEDUCTIBLE` = 1 → 7
- Total ≈ 30

Mass change: ~27 → ~30 (Δ +3). The mass rose slightly because a new invocation boundary was introduced. **Per project rules, Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP.** The split makes each function single-responsibility: `reimbursedAmount` answers "how much of the damage is covered" (the dragon/high-enchantment 50% clause logic), and `payoutForDamage` answers "payout = reimbursement minus deductible". The clause logic is now named, isolated, and reusable without the deductible entangled.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: evaluated all functions first — names already reveal intent; no renames needed.
- Rule 3 / Rule 2: extracted `reimbursedAmount` to separate the reimbursement-clause computation from deductible subtraction in `payoutForDamage`.
- Considered but rejected: merging `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5, quote surcharge) with `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (8, claim 50% clause) — these are distinct business rules; merging would be false DRY and hurt intent.
- No anticipation: the 6 remaining todos are CLI tests (`src/cli.ts`); no CLI scaffolding, parsing, or validation was added.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `reimbursedAmount` from `payoutForDamage`, isolating the 50%-clause logic from deductible subtraction (Rule 2 — single responsibility, clearer intent).
- **Mass Change**: ~27 → ~30 (Δ +3). Slight increase from the new invocation boundary; accepted because Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP per project rules.
- **Naming**: evaluated first; all names already accurate, no renames.

content.text:
All 34 tests still green.

## Step 5: APP Mass Comparison

**Before** — single `payoutForDamage`:
- Bindings: `damage`, `policy`, `damagedItem`, `item`, `halfReimbursementApplies`, `reimbursed` = 6
- Conditionals: `&&`, `?:` = 2 → mass 8
- Invocations: `find`, arrow, `enchantmentLevel`, `*`, `-` ≈ 5 → mass 10
- Constants: threshold, rate, deductible = 3
- Total ≈ 27

**After** — `reimbursedAmount` + `payoutForDamage`:
- `reimbursedAmount`: bindings 5, conditionals 2 (mass 8), invocations 4 (mass 8), constants 2 → ≈ 23
- `payoutForDamage`: binding `damage`,`policy` = 2, invocation `reimbursedAmount` + `-` = 2 (mass 4), constant `DEDUCTIBLE` = 1 → 7
- Total ≈ 30

Mass change: ~27 → ~30 (Δ +3). The mass rose slightly because a new invocation boundary was introduced. **Per project rules, Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP.** The split makes each function single-responsibility: `reimbursedAmount` answers "how much of the damage is covered" (the dragon/high-enchantment 50% clause logic), and `payoutForDamage` answers "payout = reimbursement minus deductible". The clause logic is now named, isolated, and reusable without the deductible entangled.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: evaluated all functions first — names already reveal intent; no renames needed.
- Rule 3 / Rule 2: extracted `reimbursedAmount` to separate the reimbursement-clause computation from deductible subtraction in `payoutForDamage`.
- Considered but rejected: merging `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5, quote surcharge) with `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (8, claim 50% clause) — these are distinct business rules; merging would be false DRY and hurt intent.
- No anticipation: the 6 remaining todos are CLI tests (`src/cli.ts`); no CLI scaffolding, parsing, or validation was added.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `reimbursedAmount` from `payoutForDamage`, isolating the 50%-clause logic from deductible subtraction (Rule 2 — single responsibility, clearer intent).
- **Mass Change**: ~27 → ~30 (Δ +3). Slight increase from the new invocation boundary; accepted because Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP per project rules.
- **Naming**: evaluated first; all names already accurate, no renames.
- **Tests**: All 34 passing ✅; 6 CLI todos untouched.

content.text:
All 36 tests pass, 4 todos remain untouched.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The `Step` interface adds a small fixed declaration (4 bindings ≈ 4) but the handler body lost:
- The destructuring `const { payout, remainingCap } = ...` binding pair and the object rebuild `{ payout, remainingCap }` (removed ~4 bindings + the rebuild)
- The inline type clutter

Handler body new mass ≈ 38 (down from ~42), plus the reusable `Step` interface (~4). Net handler complexity decreased; the type declaration shifts complexity into a named, reusable place that improves clarity rather than counting against the hot path.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent, the highest-clarity priority):
- Extracted a named `Step` interface, replacing the inline `{ op; items; policy; incident: any }` annotation. Removed the `any` by importing the existing `Incident` type, so the claim branch is now fully typed.
- Returned `claim(policy, step.incident)` directly instead of destructuring its result and rebuilding an identical `{ payout, remainingCap }` object. `ClaimResult` already has exactly those fields, so the destructure-and-rebuild was redundant duplication (Rule 3) — removed.

**Naming**: Evaluated first. `quotedPolicies`, `results`, `step`, `index`, `result` all reveal intent and were kept. `Step` is the only new name and reads clearly.

**Not done (correctly avoided as anticipation)**: No error/validation handling — all four `it.todo()` entries (unknown type, missing item, negative amount, over-coverage) require behavior no active test demands. Adding it would make a not-yet-active Red test pass immediately.

## Step 7: Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted named `Step` interface (typed `incident` as `Incident`, removing `any`); returned `claim(...)` result directly instead of destructuring and rebuilding an identical object.
- **Mass Change**: handler body ~42 → ~38 (removed redundant binding/rebuild); reusable `Step` declaration added separately.
- **Tests**: All 36 passing, 4 todos untouched.

The validation logic itself is unchanged (mass still ~16 inside the helper). What changed is the `quote()` body: the inline loop became a single call.

validateInsurableTypes(items);
```
- Invocations: 1 (`validateInsurableTypes`) = 2
- Bindings: 1 (`items` reference) = 1

The 5-line loop+conditional block (mass 16 in the body) is replaced by a mass-3 invocation in the `quote()` body. The validation mass is relocated, not eliminated; total program mass rose by ~3 (the extra named function declaration/binding) — but this is the deliberate Rule 2 > Rule 4 trade: the body of `quote()` is now lighter and the intent of the guard is named.

- Rule 4 trade-off acknowledged: adds one function element, justified because Rule 2 (reveals intent) outranks Rule 4 (fewest elements).

**No anticipation**: No new validation cases (item-not-in-policy, negative amount, too-many-damages) were added — those remain `it.todo()`. The refactoring only renames/relocates behavior the current `broomstick` test exercises.

The validation logic itself is unchanged (mass still ~16 inside the helper). What changed is the `quote()` body: the inline loop became a single call.

```typescript
validateInsurableTypes(items);
```
- Invocations: 1 (`validateInsurableTypes`) = 2
- Bindings: 1 (`items` reference) = 1

**Mass change**: `quote()` body guard 16 → 3 (Δ −13); program total +3 (one helper added). Net: clarity gained at the cost of one named element, consistent with the file's existing helper style.

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming/Rule 2: Extracted the anonymous validation loop in `quote()` into a named helper `validateInsurableTypes(items)`. The guard's purpose is now self-documenting and consistent with the file's existing small-helper style (`surchargeFor`, `premiumForTypeBlock`, `enchantmentLevel`).
- Rule 4 trade-off acknowledged: adds one function element, justified because Rule 2 (reveals intent) outranks Rule 4 (fewest elements).

**No anticipation**: No new validation cases (item-not-in-policy, negative amount, too-many-damages) were added — those remain `it.todo()`. The refactoring only renames/relocates behavior the current `broomstick` test exercises.

**Result**: All tests now pass (38 passing, 2 todo).
**Approach**: This is minimal — one branch on the existing `find()` miss case, no new lookup or count logic. It does not implement the negative-amount check (test 39) or the more-damages-than-insured count check (test 40), which remain todos. Prior claim tests stay green because in every one of them each damage's itemType matches an insured item (verified test 27: sword+amulet both present; test 34: two swords both present).
